questions.question: What is the primary objective of the CPR (Context, Pressure, Resolution) framework?

options.text: To sell a more expensive product.
To re-anchor the prospect to their initial pain-point while offering a low-risk micro-step to remove the specific blocker causing the stall.
To complain about the slow progress.
To ask for an early deposit.

questions.explanation: Stalls happen because the 'Effort' of moving forward suddenly feels higher than the 'Benefit'. CPR lowers the effort (Resolution) while reminding them of the benefit (Context).

questions.question: Why is offering a 'Risk-Free Pilot' or 'Sandbox' run such an effective unstaller in 2026?

options.text: Because it's a way to give away the product for free.
Because it bypasses high-friction approval processes (Legal/Security) by defining a limited, low-stakes scope where the buyer can see value before committing to the full contract.
Because it makes the prospect feel guilty if they don't buy.
To delay the close on your own terms.

questions.explanation: Modern buyers suffer from 'Commitment Phobia'. A pilot allows them to validate the product in their own environment, which often provides the evidence needed to convince the 'Shadow Committee' gatekeepers.

questions.question: What is 'Complexity Shock'?

options.text: When the product has too many features.
The feeling a buyer gets when they realize the internal work required to implement a solution is higher than their current motivation.
When the code is hard to read.
When the sales call is too technical.

questions.explanation: Complexity kills deals. If a buyer feels the project will take too much time from their team, they will stall. Your job is to simplify the implementation through automation or guided micro-steps.

questions.question: Identify a deal that is currently 'Stalled.' Apply the CPR framework. What is the specific 'Resolution' (the tiny, low-risk micro-step) you can offer the prospect to get them moving again without requiring a full contract signature today?
questions.aiPrompt: Check if the student correctly identifies the blocker (IT, Price, Complexity) and proposes a micro-step (Technical bridge, Sandbox, Case study review) that requires minimal effort from the buyer.
